paragraphs.body: It was midday on a sunny day, and my mother and I sat on the marble floor with some books and notebooks. The tiles were cool to the touch as the sun’s rays shone through the window behind me, illuminating the whole room. My mother sat in front of me as she enunciated each syllable, while I repeated after her. She would watch me as I wrote each letter slowly, scratching away at the paper with my mechanical pencil, and every now and then I would look up from my notebook to study her face to figure out if it was correct. Hurriedly erasing–blowing and brushing the eraser shavings away– and trying again. That day specifically, I got frustrated when I didn’t know what to do, and I said I hated studying. My mother looked at me and didn’t say anything for a while. I thought she was mad at me at first, but she suddenly said, “Let’s do something else.” We sat in the same spot as she painted my small fingernails with nail polish, blowing at them when she was finished with a finger. The pungent chemical smell of nail polish and acetone burned my senses and left a bitter and acrid taste in my nose and throat but yet strangely satisfying. When she was done, she held my small hands in her palms while looking at my nails. “You know, when I was a little older than you, I loved school. I loved learning new things. I loved studying. But my parents and brothers didn’t allow me to continue going to school,” she smiled as she looked at me. Bitter and sad, but a smile indeed. “When your aunts and I painted our nails, we would use knives to scratch them off before our brothers came back from school, so we wouldn’t get caught.” She was saying all this with a smile, but six-year-old me felt immense grief in her words. The sound of my aunt calling my mother broke the conversation as she got up to see what she needed. I sat there on the cool floor, the smell of nail polish engulfing the room, my mechanical pencil exactly where I left it. I couldn’t help but wonder who she would have grown to become if she had the freedom to choose.

When she was done, she held my small hands in her palms while looking at my nails. “You know, when I was a little older than you, I loved school. I loved learning new things. I loved studying. But my parents and brothers didn’t allow me to continue going to school,”

paragraphs.body: The mechanical pencil was part of a twin packet, the orange one for me and the red one for my sister. It was very intricate with lots of parts that spread out in layers. Light to hold with a silicon grip at the handle, and of course the very tiny erasure at the end that you would only have three good uses for, usually reserved for emergencies. I would put several leads in the middle cylinder and watch them swish around in the clear tube, clashing against one another waiting to be next in line. The pencil was once clear and bright, but with time the lead residue colored all the internal parts in graphite, stuck to all the internal parts forever. The eraser was reduced to black matter, and the silicon part was now sticky and a nuisance rather than an aid. When I tried to clean the silicone grip, I only managed to break it as the silicon disintegrated with a single wipe.

annotations.comment: This is a great description, but--interestingly--have you already set up the nail polish as an odd object?
annotations.context_left: three good uses for, usually reserved for emergencies. I would put several leads in the middle

paragraphs.body: The same pen she gave me when I was younger became the same one she disdained for letting me hold. She gave me a weapon and told me to protect myself but she didn’t imagine that it would be used against her ideals. My mother was always delicate, soft, and kind–Perfect mother and wife characteristics–yet she would abandon all of that for me and my sister, or at least the life she envisioned for us. “We didn’t give up everything for you to go live out on your own.” You gave up everything for me to get a better education, and I delivered by getting into a top university. “You should think of marriage, you are not getting any younger.” But I’m only 19 and he is 27. “Once you are married, you can go wherever you want.” How different would that make me from a cattle? My mother gave me a weapon and told to me to not use it. She had given younger me a pen and pointed to the sky and told me to dream. But not too high where she can’t see. Not too high where men wouldn’t see. Not too high where marriage can’t be seen. Just like her mother sat by while she fought tooth and nail to continue school, she sat by while she saw me do the same.

paragraphs.body: When my grandmother passed, my mother and aunts grieved immensely. They grieved for 40 days, and never shed another tear about her in front of us again. She would do a memorial every year with food, but nothing more nothing less. There is no specific word for grief in our language, it is simply called sadness. For her, sadness was only reserved for the dead, for those who breathed the same air as us, ate food on the same table, and slept beside us. My mother has only mourned and grieved the dead, but of herself that dreamt of being something other than a cattle with the noose passed from one man to another. All she could do was bury it deep in her heart and hope no noise would come from the attic. Only to come face to face with an incarnation of her dreams reflected through me.

For her, sadness was only reserved for the dead, for those who breathed the same air as us, ate food on the same table, and slept beside us.

paragraphs.body: When I first learned of the word, I thought it was foreign to me. I am thankful I didn’t experience the loss of a loved one. So I thought I could not possibly know what that could have felt for me. But it was not foreign at all, it was all too familiar. The definition of grief comes from Early 13c., "hardship, suffering, pain, bodily affliction," from Old French grief "wrong, grievance, injustice, misfortune, calamity" (13c.), from grever "afflict, burden, oppress," from Latin gravare "make heavy; cause grief," from gravis "weighty" (from PIE root *gwere- (1) "heavy"). Meaning "mental pain, sorrow" is from c. 1300. Good grief as an exclamation of surprise, dismay, etc., is from 1912. (Etymology.) The word grief was first recorded around 1200, stemming from the Latin verb gravāre, meaning “to burden,” from gravis, “heavy” (dictionary.com.). The word was in high use in the early 1800s and saw a steady decrease in usage frequency with the lowest being 5.251 per million in 1978. The word grief has since increased steadily in usage since the very low hit it took in the 1920s to 1990s. (Etymology.) But to me, grief is the lingering ghost of love, the cold smoke left behind by a put-out flame. It is the remaining of love. Grief is not just reserved for the dead and departed. Grief is not just ceaseless sadness and sorrow. Grief is not what is buried and covered in dirt. It is the perseverance of tenderness and affection. It is when the world cruelly takes a part of you, and you are only left with a body that keeps going but no soul. It’s when the love you hold in your arms has nowhere to go, only to turn into a bile of sorrows that just becomes harder and harder to hold onto. It is the act of worshiping a dead god. I call out to the heavens, but the sky is empty. Grief is not what is six feet under, but it is what continues walking, eating, and sleeping alongside us, though none else is able to see it. Like a ghost haunting our days and asking to not be forgotten. It is what we shove inside our hearts, hoping it will beat again, only for it to bleed over everything.

annotations.comment: There's some beautiful language in this paragraph, but let's talk about the potential power of the definition paragraph to point your reader to your essay's central focusing idea. Is this paragraph doing that? Is it defining the "right" things?
annotations.context_left: sadness and sorrow. Grief is not what is buried and covered in dirt. It is the perseverance of

paragraphs.body: My mother’s grief was only making her more anxious about our separation, and in doing so she began repeating the same cycle that women in our culture have experienced for generations. We have experienced grief for the things we lost before we could even name them. The feeling has existed without being acknowledged or seen by anyone but shared by many women. The only thing they knew how to do was bury it and hope their daughters won’t face the same pain. Even if they have to paint them in graphite in doing so.

The only thing they knew how to do was bury it and hope their daughters won’t face the same pain. Even if they have to paint them in graphite in doing so.
